title: The 11 Best Telehealth Platforms
subtitle: A ranked list of the top telehealth solutions for healthcare providers, focusing on EHR integration, patient experience, and HIPAA compliance.

editor.name: Top 11 Editorial
editor.credential: Autonomous AI ranking engine — methodology v1.0 weights public
editor.conflict_disclosure: None. The editor of Top 11 is not a candidate on this list.

independence.statement: Top 11 takes no payment from any provider on this list. Scores are computed from a public weighted rubric; methodology weights were locked before entry research began.

changelog.text: Initial publication. Methodology v1.0 weights HIPAA Compliance (25%), EHR Integration (25%), Patient Experience (20%), Provider Features (15%), Pricing (10%), and Support (5%).

answer_capsule: The best telehealth platform for most providers is Doxy.me for its simplicity and free tier, followed closely by Teladoc Health for enterprise scale and Amwell for its comprehensive service offerings.

criteria.name: HIPAA_Compliance_and_Security
criteria.description: Strength of security protocols, HIPAA/HITECH compliance, BAA availability, and data encryption standards.

criteria.name: EHR_Integration_and_Workflow
criteria.description: Depth and breadth of integrations with major Electronic Health Record systems and seamless fit into clinical workflows.

criteria.name: Patient_Experience_and_Accessibility
criteria.description: Ease of use for patients, no-download options, multi-language support, and reliability of the connection.

criteria.name: Provider_Features_and_Usability
criteria.description: Quality of the clinician interface, including features like virtual waiting rooms, charting, e-prescribing, and billing tools.

criteria.name: Pricing_and_Scalability
criteria.description: Transparency, value for money, availability of a free or low-cost tier, and ability to scale with a growing practice.

criteria.name: Support_and_Implementation
criteria.description: Quality of customer support, availability of training resources, and ease of initial setup and onboarding.

solves: Integrated billing and telehealth for therapists

guide.a: Before choosing a platform, map out your ideal virtual visit workflow. Do you need deep EHR integration for charting, or a simple, standalone video tool? This decision will narrow your options significantly.
The best platform is useless if patients can't easily use it. Look for browser-based solutions that don't require app downloads, and test the interface from a patient's perspective.

Never assume a video tool is compliant. Insist on a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA) from the provider. This is a non-negotiable legal requirement for handling Protected Health Information (PHI).

how_to_choose: Determine if you need a standalone platform or one integrated with your existing EHR/practice management software.
Evaluate the total cost of ownership, including per-provider fees, per-visit charges, and implementation costs.
Request a demo and run a trial with both clinical staff and a test patient to assess usability for all parties.
Check for features specific to your specialty, such as group therapy rooms for behavioral health or device streaming for cardiology.

faqs.q: What is the difference between telehealth and telemedicine?
faqs.a: Telemedicine specifically refers to remote clinical services, while telehealth is a broader term that includes non-clinical services like provider training, administrative meetings, and continuing medical education, in addition to clinical services.

faqs.q: How much does a telehealth platform typically cost?
faqs.a: Costs vary widely. Some platforms like Doxy.me offer a free, basic tier. Paid plans for solo practitioners typically range from $30 to $100 per provider per month. Enterprise solutions for hospitals can cost thousands per month, often with implementation fees.

faqs.q: Is Zoom or Google Meet sufficient for telehealth?
faqs.a: Special healthcare-specific versions of these platforms (Zoom for Healthcare, Google Workspace with a BAA) can be HIPAA-compliant for basic video calls. However, they lack the integrated clinical workflows, EHR connections, and patient management features of dedicated telehealth platforms.

faqs.q: What are the key features to look for in a telehealth platform?
faqs.a: Key features include HIPAA compliance with a BAA, a virtual waiting room, EHR integration, e-prescribing, secure messaging, and an easy-to-use interface for both patients and providers.

honest_disclosures: This list focuses on platforms for providers to connect with their own patients; it does not cover direct-to-consumer services where patients find a new provider through the platform.
Most candidates are US-based, and while many can be used internationally, the feature sets (especially EHR integrations and e-prescribing) are optimized for the US healthcare market.
Pricing for enterprise-level platforms is often opaque and requires a custom quote, so listed price ranges are estimates.

glossary.definition: The ability of a telehealth platform to connect and share data with a practice's Electronic Health Record (EHR) system. This can range from simple demographic data sync to deep integration where visit notes and billing information are automatically filed in the patient's chart.

entries.name: Doxy.me
entries.best_for: Solo practitioners and small clinics needing a simple, secure, and free or low-cost solution for video visits without complex integrations.
entries.verdict: Doxy.me ranks first for its unparalleled simplicity and robust free tier, making secure, browser-based telehealth accessible to any practice, regardless of size or budget.
entries.verdict_short: The top pick for its unmatched simplicity, security, and powerful free version for any practice size.
entries.praise: The platform's 'no download required' approach for patients creates a nearly frictionless experience, significantly lowering barriers to care.
entries.praise_short: Extremely easy for patients to use with no downloads.
entries.criticism: Its strength in simplicity means it lacks deep EHR integrations and the advanced practice management features found in more comprehensive suites.
entries.criticism_short: Lacks deep EHR integration and advanced features.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-05-31.

entries.name: Teladoc Health (Solo Platform for Providers)
entries.best_for: Large health systems and hospitals requiring a scalable, enterprise-grade platform with a wide range of specialty-specific hardware and clinical services.
entries.verdict: Teladoc Health is the best choice for large-scale healthcare organizations due to its market-leading enterprise platform, deep integration capabilities, and comprehensive clinical program support.
entries.verdict_short: The leading enterprise platform for large health systems needing scalability and deep integration.
entries.praise: The platform's purpose-built hardware and software for various specialties, from tele-ICU to dermatology, are unmatched in the industry.
entries.criticism: Its cost and complexity make it inaccessible and overkill for small practices, and the focus is squarely on large, system-level deployments.
entries.criticism_short: Too complex and expensive for small practices.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-05-31.

entries.name: Amwell
entries.best_for: Health systems and payers looking for a highly configurable and comprehensive telehealth ecosystem that bridges virtual and in-person care.
entries.verdict: Amwell secures a top spot with its powerful, modular platform that allows large organizations to build and scale diverse telehealth programs across the entire care continuum.
entries.verdict_short: A powerful, modular platform for large organizations to scale diverse telehealth programs.
entries.praise: Its strong partnerships with major health plans and deep integration with EHRs like Epic make it a seamless choice for established healthcare players.
entries.praise_short: Strong payer partnerships and deep EHR integration.
entries.criticism: Like other enterprise leaders, Amwell's platform is not designed for small, independent practices, and its implementation can be a lengthy and resource-intensive process.
entries.criticism_short: Not for small practices; implementation is lengthy.
sources_pending: Gartner Magic Quadrant for Virtual Care

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-05-31.

entries.name: SimplePractice
entries.best_for: Behavioral health professionals and other wellness practitioners in private practice who want an all-in-one practice management solution.
entries.verdict: SimplePractice excels by tightly integrating telehealth into a complete practice management suite, making it the best choice for therapists and solo practitioners who need scheduling, billing, and notes in one place.
entries.verdict_short: The best all-in-one suite (telehealth, billing, notes) for therapists and solo practitioners.
entries.praise: Its streamlined workflow, from client booking on a professional website to automated billing and reminders, saves practitioners significant administrative time.
entries.criticism: The platform is highly specialized for behavioral health and wellness, making it a poor fit for other medical specialties, and it doesn't integrate with external EHRs.
entries.criticism_short: Poor fit for medical specialties outside behavioral health.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-05-31.

entries.name: Zoom for Healthcare
entries.best_for: Practices and health systems that prioritize familiarity and ease of use, and have the IT resources to integrate it into their existing clinical workflows.
entries.verdict: Zoom for Healthcare makes the list because its ubiquitous, user-friendly interface ensures high adoption rates among patients and providers, offering a reliable video connection with the necessary security layers for healthcare.
entries.verdict_short: A top choice for its familiar interface and reliability, ensuring high patient/provider adoption.
entries.praise: The video and audio quality are consistently best-in-class, providing a stable and clear connection crucial for clinical consultations.
entries.criticism: Out of the box, it's just a video tool; it lacks a virtual waiting room, patient scheduling, and other clinical features unless integrated with another application via its marketplace.
entries.criticism_short: Lacks key clinical features without third-party integrations.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-05-31.

entries.name: Updox
entries.best_for: Small to medium-sized practices seeking a unified communication platform that combines telehealth with secure text, patient reminders, and electronic fax.
entries.verdict: Updox earns its spot by positioning telehealth as one component of a complete patient communication strategy, with strong EHR integrations that help streamline front-office and clinical workflows.
entries.verdict_short: A complete communication platform with strong EHR integrations to streamline practice workflows.
entries.praise: Its extensive library of pre-built integrations with over 100 EHRs makes it one of the easiest platforms to add onto an existing practice tech stack.
entries.praise_short: Excellent pre-built integrations with many EHRs.
entries.criticism: The user interface can feel dated compared to more modern, video-first platforms, and some users report the feature set can be overwhelming.
entries.criticism_short: User interface feels dated and can be overwhelming.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-05-31.

entries.name: Mend
entries.best_for: Practices focused on reducing no-show rates and automating patient intake through smart scheduling and communication tools.
entries.verdict: Mend stands out for its intelligent patient engagement features, using AI-powered reminders and digital intake forms to dramatically reduce no-shows and prepare patients for their virtual visits.
entries.verdict_short: Reduces no-shows effectively with AI-powered reminders and streamlined digital intake forms.
entries.praise: The platform's ability to automatically send appointment links and handle forms ahead of time significantly reduces administrative burden and improves clinic efficiency.
entries.praise_short: Automated forms and links reduce administrative work.
entries.criticism: The core video consultation interface is less feature-rich than some competitors, as the platform's main focus is on the surrounding patient journey.
entries.criticism_short: Core video interface is less feature-rich.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-05-31.

entries.name: VSee
entries.best_for: Organizations needing a highly customizable and flexible telehealth solution, from simple video calls to complex, multi-party workflows.
entries.verdict: VSee is a veteran in the telehealth space, offering a uniquely configurable platform that can be adapted for a wide variety of clinical use cases, famously including providing support to astronauts on the ISS.
entries.verdict_short: A highly configurable veteran platform adaptable to diverse and complex clinical use cases.
entries.praise: Its low-bandwidth video technology ensures a stable connection even in areas with poor internet, a critical feature for expanding access to care.
entries.criticism: The user experience requires a software download for full functionality, which can be a barrier for less tech-savvy patients compared to browser-based rivals.
entries.criticism_short: Requires a software download, a barrier for some patients.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-05-31.

entries.name: Tebra (formerly Kareo)
entries.best_for: Independent medical practices already using or considering the Tebra (Kareo) ecosystem for practice management, billing, and EHR.
entries.verdict: Tebra's telehealth solution is a logical and powerful choice for small to medium-sized independent practices, as it's fully integrated within its widely-used practice management and clinical software.
entries.verdict_short: A powerful, fully integrated telehealth option for practices using the Tebra/Kareo suite.
entries.praise: The seamless flow from scheduling a telehealth visit to charting and billing within a single system is a major efficiency gain for small practice staff.
entries.praise_short: Seamless workflow from scheduling to billing.
entries.criticism: As a feature of a larger platform, it lacks the advanced capabilities of standalone, best-of-breed telehealth solutions and is not a viable option for practices not using Tebra's core software.
entries.criticism_short: Not a standalone option; lacks advanced features.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-05-31.

entries.name: athenaTelehealth
entries.best_for: Medical groups and practices deeply embedded in the athenahealth ecosystem who want a native, fully integrated telehealth solution.
entries.verdict: athenaTelehealth is the definitive choice for practices on the athenaOne platform, offering unmatched integration that embeds virtual visits directly into existing charting, scheduling, and billing workflows.
entries.verdict_short: The go-to choice for athenaOne users, with unmatched workflow and billing integration.
entries.praise: Because it's a native module, providers can conduct a video visit while simultaneously documenting in the patient's chart within the same window, a major workflow advantage.
entries.praise_short: Video and charting in a single, seamless window.
entries.criticism: The solution is only available to athenahealth customers, and its feature set is basic compared to standalone market leaders.
entries.criticism_short: Only for athenahealth customers; basic features.

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-05-31.

entries.name: Spruce Health
entries.best_for: Modern practices that want to unify all patient and team communication—including phone, text, fax, and video—into a single, mobile-first application.
entries.verdict: Our wildcard, Spruce Health, isn't just a telehealth platform but a comprehensive, HIPAA-compliant communication hub that treats video visits as one of many ways to connect with patients, reflecting a shift towards more flexible, asynchronous care models.
entries.verdict_short: A communication-first platform where video is one piece of a unified patient conversation.
entries.praise: Its ability to manage a practice's phone system, secure messaging, and video calls from a single app is a game-changer for mobile-first clinicians and distributed teams.
entries.praise_short: Manages phone, text, and video in one app.
entries.criticism: The platform's telehealth features are more basic than dedicated solutions, and it lacks the deep EHR workflow integrations of its larger competitors.
entries.criticism_short: Basic telehealth features and limited EHR integration.
sources_pending: Forbes review of healthcare communication apps

risk_signals.summary: No material public risk signals as of 2026-05-31.
